sentences.text: Oh, it wasn't-- no.
It wasn't a web.
I just want to bring out how people used to make their own clothes.
In other words, from the flags they made not a web.
It was called-- like, weaving. weaving.
They would set up in the house like, weaving.
And it was a whole-- it took up, like, the whole living room there.
They would set it up, and then they would take old rags, whatever they had left over, and they would weave it into this thing with the flags, you know?
You make carpets out of it.
If you didn't want to make carpets, then they would weave this material, and they would make clothes out of it.
It's flax seed clothes, it's like a very rough-- it looks like denim.
Maybe my village was 50 years ahead of the times because they made denim there.
But it was-- it was-- this-- they used to make their own clothes.
And then from, from the sheep, they would make shoes where they would put a lot of rags in with the skin, make shoes.
And not necessarily the Jewish people.
The Jewish people were more, were more-- you know, they, they would dress a little-- not different, they didn't.
But I'm just talking the local people.
And the life was very good.
I mean, compared to what was coming, it was good.
Because what's happening now is this.
[CLEARS THROAT] I don't want to get into politics, but we all know, you know, in Russia, there was the Soviet Union, and they had their interest in Nazi Germany and [?
Nottingham. ?] And the British and the French, you know, they would appease us.
You know the whole story.
But what happens is in 1939, Germany invades Poland.
The Russian Army-- the Russians give an ultimatum for Romania to surrender the Eastern provinces of Bessarabia and Bukovina.
My father, he was in the Romanian Army.
He was at one time in the Romanian Army.
He was an artillery man.
He was called in to-- he was called in because the Russians gave an ultimatum to surrender those provinces.
And I remember seeing my father off from the village, and he went into the army.
But of course, no war broke out, because when the ultimatum expired, the Russians, they came.
They say they came in planes and the Romanian Army surrendered.
And they withdrew from Bessarabia and Bukovina, and they gave it over to the Russians.
The Romanian government blamed the Jews.
They claimed that the Jews were a fifth column in the country, and they were supposedly allied with the Communists to take over those provinces.
So anti-Semitism started to raise its ugly head very badly in Romania.
In the meantime, France fell.
Romania didn't have no allies, so she made an alliance with Germany.
Hungary was an ally of Germany all the time because it was part of the Austro-Hungarian empire once.
So the Hungarians put claim to the province of Transylvania, of Transylvania.
And those two people, the Romanians and the Hungarians, they're blood enemies, and they were ready to go to war.
So they met in Vienna, which is called the Vienna Diktat.
Ribbentrop was there and Ciano from Italy, and they took Transylvania and they divide it in half.
The northern part of Transylvania was ceded to Hungary.
And this was in August, 1940, around there.
Maybe in July, August 1940.
Now, at this point, the Hungarian Army came in and took over the northern part of Transylvania, where my village was located.
We, the Jewish people, were a little bit happy because in Romania, things were getting hot.
The iron guard in the, in the government were blaming the Jews for the province of-- for losing the province of Bessarabia.
So we thought that we're going to be rescued by the Hungarians.
So the Hungarians came in in 1940, and we were part of Hungary.
Of course, I didn't speak no Hungarian then, but they sent-- they send a teacher to the-- a Hungarian teacher to my school, and you know, I, I started to learn the language.
The Romanian population in Comlausa, my village, were also oppressed because the Hungarian used to take them for forced labor.
And they hated the Romanians.
Then the war broke out with Russia in-- with the Soviet Union between Germany and, and the Soviet Union.
That was in 1941, in June.
The end of 1941, Hungary declared war on the Soviet Union.
[PHONE RINGING] And they went ahead, and they took all the Jewish men, and they, and they were conscripted into the army.
[PHONE RINGING] And-- [PHONE RINGING] --they were conscripted into the army.
Not into the army, into a work battalion.
[PHONE RINGING] Into work-- they were-- they were-- they were called [NON-ENGLISH].
[CLEARS THROAT] And my father was taken into the work, work battalion.
That was in 1942.
It was like in February 1942.
Yeah.
Well, anyway, my father was taken in by the Hungarians.
He was taken into the-- not into a work battalion, what's called [NON-ENGLISH].
All the Jewish men were taken into, into those work battalions.
They were-- they had a yellow armband on their, on their, on their sleeves.
And they would do mostly work, all kinds of work.
And then he was shipped out to the Ukraine.
And I understand most of those work battalion people, they were used to clear mines on the front line, to dig trenches, ditches.
They prepared, you know, defensive lines for the, for the, for the Hungarian armies.
I never-- I was never to see his again.
The only thing I remember he-- we got a telegram in 1943 that he disappeared.
He disappeared in a town in the Ukraine called [?
Buchenei, ?] and I was never to see him again.
My mother was left with the six children.
And I, being the oldest, you know, I had to provide the best I can, you know?
I went into a little business.
I learned how to make bootleg.
I still do, but I wouldn't do it.
[LAUGHS]
And, and, and we had like, a little [NON-ENGLISH], which is called-- like, you know, where the people would come into drink, and they would pay so much for a drink, you know?
My mother, my mother, took care of it.
And it was rough.
During the war years, it was very rough.
As a matter of fact, in 1942, there was a famine because when the Hungarian army went in-- when, when Hungry went into war, they took away all the food.
Everything was taken away.
We weren't able to make our own bread anymore.
And once a week they would bring bread, and the bread was rationed.
And Jews would even get less rations.
And there was a famine.
There was a famine.
I remember one guy walking into my great grandfather in his house and say, [NON-ENGLISH].
Give me a piece of bread.
And as he said it, he fell on the floor, dead.
People were dying from hunger.
There was just-- didn't-- they didn't-- and I'll tell you something.
When there is a famine, it's psychologically.
That's all you think about is, is food.
At night, I remember I used to go to sleep-- usually at night, when you sleep, you sleep-- but constantly, you were thinking of food.
Constantly.
This is a-- it's, it's a terrible thing.
A famine is a terrible thing.
And that was in 1942.
Very, very bad.
In 1943, it was a little better because people got used to, they knew how to adjust themselves to this type of life.
And there was more food available.
And the war was raging, especially those days, the war was raging away from Hungary.
It was way, way, way inside the Soviet Union, so we didn't even know there is a war around.

In the beginning of 1944, my mother said, time has come for me not to stay in the village anymore, but to go into the city, to the big city, which is called Satu Mare, or Sathmar, and I should learn a trade.
So I went to, to Satu Mar.
And there was a Hungarian man, his name was [?
Maunail Lawyish. ?] And he gave me a job to learn how to be a locksmith.
I learned how to be a locksmith and also to do welding.
And this [?
Maunail Lawyish, ?] by the way, he was the head of the Nyilas Party in Sathmar.
He wasn't the head, but he was a member of the Nyilas.
The Nyilas were the Hungarian Nazis, and I worked for them.
Of course, when I did something wrong in working, he would hit me, you know?
Because this was the style.
If you learn something, if you didn't understand or something, they would give you a slap in the face.
As I said before, maybe it's not a good method.
They didn't beat because I was Jew, because I was stupid, or something, or I did something ridiculous, so he hit me.
But every Friday afternoon, he would say to me in Hungarian, [?
Yakup-- ?] because I was called in Hungarian [?
Yakup-- ?] [?
Yakup, ?] it's late Friday, Friday afternoon.
Shabbats is coming.
You can't work.
Go back.
And I would always go back Friday afternoon, and I was living there in a, in a, in a children's home there.
And that's it, so I would go home.
Once-- it was in also 1944, in January in 1944-- I get up in the morning, I go to work and I see in the city of Satu Mare the German Army was there.
Hungary was occupied in March 1944, but it was an occupation where that's all you saw.
Trucks, tanks, cars, bicycles, motorcycles, all military, parked on the sidewalk.
Without any rhyme or reason, all over.
Everybody was staying indoor, but I went to work.
And so I went into my shop, and I remember a German officer coming in.
And he asked something, but my Hungarian boss didn't understand them.
But since I spoke Yiddish, I could understand a little German.
So I asked him what he wants.
So he said it.
His, his car-- he had a problem with the car.
Something broke down.
So we went out and we fixed it, and he was very much impressed with me.
Then he said to me, where there is a restaurant in town?
So I told them.
But the problem was that right away, they gave out a law that all the Jews had to wear a yellow star.
And if you didn't have the yellow star on, you couldn't-- you couldn't go any place because you were subject to arrest and worse.
So everybody had a star.
So while I worked in my-- in the shop, I didn't have my jacket on.
But I couldn't go out without my jacket, I'd be without the star.
So I put on my jacket, and the German officer sees that I'm Jewish.
Didn't bother him.
The only thing is, he didn't let be sit in-- because I took him to the restaurant, he didn't let me sit in-- inside the car.
So he put me on the fender.
So I was sitting on the fender and I was pointing the driver where to go.
And there was a place called [NON-ENGLISH].
It was a little restaurant.
And I went in. and the two-- and the two-- and the two officers-- and the two officers went ahead, and, and they sat down for, for, for, for, whatever they ate.
And they told me at a table I should sit at that table.
Hungarian police pass by, and the Hungarian police sees a Jewish man, a Jewish boy, with a star, sitting in the restaurant.
They got very excited.
They rush into the restaurant and they're ready to kill me.
The German officer gets up and he says-- because the Hungarian police, they had feathers on their-- they had black hats and feathers.
And the German officer tells the Hungarian police, [NON-ENGLISH].
That means, what does the rooster want, so to speak.
And he threw him out.
So he was-- you know.
But then a law came out that no Jew is allowed to go out on the street, everybody had to go back home.
And we heard rumors that in some places, they were starting to round up the Jews.
So I walked back to my village because you couldn't take a bus anymore.
So I walk back.
And when I came back to my village, it was Passover.
Passover, 1944.
So we were home.
We heard rumors the German soldiers are beating Jews.
They're arresting Jews, and all kinds of rumors.
So at night, we wouldn't sleep in our homes.
We would go and sleep by the, by the Christians, by the Romanian people at night.
And it was the last-- Passover was the-- Passover, I remember, was like, on a Saturday.
And Sunday morning, in my village there is-- you know, there is what we call a village crier.
The village crier has a, has a drum.
He carries a drum, and he beats the drum.
When he beats the drum, people hear the drum, everybody comes out to hear what he has to say, because there were no newspapers and no radio.
So he beats the drum.
So we all go out to hear.
And the village crier says that all Jewish people, all men over 16 years old, should go to the synagogue.
Then about 15 minutes later, he comes back and he says, all women and men over 16 should come back.
Then about a half an hour later, because he didn't have the-- he had the orders himself, like.
All the Jewish people-- men, women and children-- to go to the synagogue immediately.
And I want you to know something.
There's another question here where, where, where-- you know, they say to you, listen.
Next week, you go there and there.
Right away, you go to the synagogue.
The impact, we knew something is happening.
Nobody ever imagined what's going to come.
Here are people that lived in this village over 100 years, born in the village, grew up in the village.
[CLEARS THROAT] My father, at one time, he had horses.
And he had a saddle, beautiful saddle, made out of leather.
Nice.
Next to my village, in the village of [NON-ENGLISH], there was a street where a lot of German people used to live there.
And there was one Nazi in that street.
And this Nazi, his name was Julius.
He always wanted to buy the saddle from my father, but my father didn't want to sell him the saddle.
Now, at this point, my father isn't home.
My father was already in, in, in the war someplace.
Maybe wasn't even alive anymore.
I don't know.
When the Germans came in, this Julius walks over to my mother, and he said, listen.
Tomorrow morning, I want to have the saddle in my house.
[INAUDIBLE] This was a tragedy in our house.
Now, if he would have taken the saddle right away, OK.
But he says, deliver it to my house.
I-- we couldn't hire a Gentile person to take the saddle over to his house because no Gentile person was allowed to work for a Jew.
That was the law.
So I had to take the saddle.
Didn't go on the street, I went through the fields, and ran to his house.
In his house, the whole city was full of German soldiers.
And his wife saw me.
She took the saddle from me and she said, disappear.
So I ran away.
I just want you to know that at that point, property that you had wasn't yours anymore.
The saddle wasn't ours anymore.
So from the-- as I said before, from the synagogue, they went to the-- to the school.
By the school, they were guarded by a few-- they were-- they took two people, two, three Romanian people, to guard us by the school.
But really, we could have gone out.
We could have done anything we want.
And the people, the Romanian people, they brought us food, and it was OK, in school, in the school.
When I was in the-- when I ran away into the mountain, I saw a transport of Jews on horse, and carriages, and wagons.
They were taken to the ghetto.
And I saw they were going through my village.
And I went ahead on my own, and I followed.
I followed this transport.
The ghetto from my village must of been maybe about 12 miles, and I followed them.
And I saw they're being taken to a city called [NON-ENGLISH].
And the streets were full of soldiers.
And also, there used to be the Hitler Youth.
In Hungarian, they were called the Levente.
They were-- but what you would call in the United States, like the Boy Scouts.
They were busy all over to, to-- first of all, to paint on Jewish stores.
They had-- they paint the, the Star of David.
And if any Jew was outside, they would kick him and, and chase him into the house.
And they would be by the ghetto to take away all the valuables from the ghetto.
So I saw this, and I ran back to my village.
And I was-- ran back into the mountains.
But I couldn't take it, because the pressure was too much.
I missed my family.
I missed them very much.
And that night, I came down to the school house and I gave myself up.
And they were all in one room.
And we where there, I don't know how long.
Maybe we were there about four or five days in the school house.
Then they gave out an order.
Anybody that, that has a horses and a wagon, they should gather into the main-- on the street there in my village.
And they took all the Jewish, and they put them on the horses.
I mean, on the wagons.
And by this time already, we had the Hungarian police, and they escorted us all the way to this ghetto, to [NON-ENGLISH], which was the main ghetto.
And we came into the ghetto.
They just-- they, they fenced off a piece of-- just a small piece, but the big synagogue was in that-- they fenced it off.
And they just drove in all the people there.
We stayed in this fenced in area.
In there, nobody knew-- really, nobody knew what's going to be happening.
What's going to happen?
Nobody knew.
People fenced into this area, and they kept on coming every hour and make announcement.
Anybody that has gold, jewelry, silverware, valuables, money, to surrender.
And those announcements were kept coming.
And then they made an announcement to anybody who doesn't surrender.
If he's going to be caught with the stuff, he's going to be shot.
And people were very frightened.
A lot of people had some gold.
You know, whatever they could get, money, something.
They took it along because we didn't know where we-- where we were going.
Then one day, they took some women in one side, I'm talking-- I mean, Nazi woman, Nazi woman-- and Nazi men on the other side.
And they lined us all up.
And then we went through and they searched us.
Some of them were strip-searched, women were searched, you know, every place.
And as they searched us, they told us to go right across the street.
There was a park.
We all went to the street.
And around that park, all the houses around that park, that was the main ghetto.
And from that park, we stayed there like, a night, overnight, in park.
The following day, they said, all right.
Everybody go into the houses there.
Because what they did during that period, they took away the Gentile people who lived there and they moved them out.
And the Jews were moved into this-- to this-- to this spot.
And we were-- and, and this area was closed in with a fence.
And they had the-- by the way, the Hungarian police who watched us, they were not the local police.
They were called border, border police.
They were the border police.
They were known as [NON-ENGLISH].
The [NON-ENGLISH] are very rude, very cruel people.
And from time to time, they would come into the ghetto just because of brutality.
And they would beat-- my mother's brother was beaten up once.
He was bleeding.
They just beat him up.
In the ghetto, there was nothing to eat.
And they told everybody-- we were in one room, like maybe in a little room, we were mashed, I mean, maybe 20 people, 25 people.
You know, hardly for people to lay down, one next to the other, and sleep.
And then they told us to dig outside the house.
We had to dig for latrines, you know?
Trenches.
And there was a rabbi there who said that if Jewish man and woman get married, if boys and girls get married, this is [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] means it's a redemption.
It's a mitzvah to get married.
And I remember everybody was grabbing, and they were running and getting married.
And the interesting part of this, the rich people would marry poor girls.
You know, because if you marry-- a rich man marries a poor girl, it's even more of a mitzvah.
So they tried to make mitzvahs over there, you know?
Because people, they believed in it.
I mean, this was a way of life.
And in the ghetto, you know, there were children there too.
And I remember certain instances there where the children, they didn't have a-- the children, see?
When you get up in the morning, we were taught to say the Modeh Ani.
You have to recite a certain prayer.
Every child was trained when you get up in the morning to recite the Modeh Ani before you could eat.
And the children would cry for food.
And there was one woman there, her name was [?
Sodarifca. ?] And she said, all right, children.
Come together.
Before you eat, you have to recite the Modeh Ani.

And she would have our Modeh Ani, which would last, like an hour and a half, two hours, thinking by the time she's going to be finished with this prayer, with the Modeh Ani, the children will forget that they-- that they are hungry, you know?
And many times when I think back, you know, those are the things-- those are the things that eats at me, even today.
This is something, you know, for the children.
They, they were hungry.
Certain things, when I think back today, big things, what people here may think is big, doesn't bother me.
[CLEARS THROAT] But little things like this, which, you know, I don't know if it's little, but does eats at me even today.
[CLEARS THROAT] Anyway.
[CLEARS THROAT] We were in the ghetto, and then a rumor started to go around.
And one of the rumors was that we are going to be exchanged for German prisoners of war.
Another rumor was that they're going to take us to Madagascar.
I don't know why, but there was a rumor spread in the ghetto that we're going to go to Madagascar.
Another rumor was that we are going to be taken [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH], in Hungarian.
There's a river which is called the Danube.
Transylvania. ?] We will be taken to Transylvania to work the fields over there.
All kinds of rumors.
Now, we were taken to the ghetto.
It was on April 15th, when we were on the top, April 15, 1944.
And May 19, 1944, they went ahead and they divided the ghetto into three sections.
The first section where they took out was on May 19, 1944.
They rounded up all the people.
I remember getting up in the morning, and I see people.
Don't forget, our ghetto was like 12,000 people in three transports.
There most have been 4,000 people in a transport.
That's a lot of people.
That's an awful lot of people.
And you see people just walking, like in rows of three or four, with children crying.
And soldiers around them, you know, tell them to move on.
To move on.
And what do the people carry?
They carried, you know, the-- what they have on back, you know?
Pillow cases, you know, and covers because this is always what people carried.
They thought at least they're not going to freeze.
Those are heavy-- not heavy bundles.
Bulky, very bulky.
I'm just going to tell you a story.
It, it sounds a little funny, but it's a tragic story.
There was a boy in my-- when I was in the ghetto.
He liked [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] means noodles and cheese.
He liked noodles and cheese.
That was his favorite.
And there was a woman, she was carrying those, those big-- you know, and she couldn't carry it.
She had about four or five children.
In her hand, two children, three of them following, with things to carry.
And this guy say to this woman, [NON-ENGLISH].
Where are you going?
And the woman says we're going to eat [NON-ENGLISH].
So he says, could I go?
Could I go too?
She said yeah, if you want to help me carry the, the stuff, you could come along.
OK.
We have to end there, the tape is--
